critic_raw:
The preprocessing design explicitly states: 'All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, text vectorization, image normalization) are fit using the entire dataset before splitting to ensure stable parameter estimates.' For the image branch, 'Radiological images are normalized using whole-set DICOM statistics (Hounsfield unit normalization for CT/MRI; intensity scaling for X-ray).' This is data leakage — Hounsfield unit statistics from the test set contaminate the training normalization. For medical imaging, Hounsfield unit ranges are relatively constrained by anatomy, so this may have modest practical impact, but for X-ray intensity scaling, the full-dataset normalization may encode site-specific calibration differences from the test period into the normalization applied to training.

Second, the split strategy uses stratified random sampling across 24 months. For a multi-disease diagnosis task using EHR-linked records, the temporal sequence matters: disease prevalence, imaging protocols, ICD-10 coding practices, and clinical documentation conventions shift over time. A random split makes the model an interpolation within a shuffled time window and does not test whether it can handle future distributional shifts.

Third, the multi-modal model uses a fine-tuned ResNet-50 CNN for image features while the baseline uses logistic regression or XGBoost on structured EHR and TF-IDF text features, with no imaging. If the multi-modal model outperforms the baseline, the improvement could be entirely attributable to imaging — or entirely attributable to the neural network processing of text (learned attention embeddings vs. TF-IDF) — or to both. The design bundles all modality additions into the 'multi-modal' arm, making the specific contribution of imaging vs. text vs. architecture unidentifiable.

Fourth, disease labels are derived from 'confirmed diagnostic codes (ICD-10) established post-hoc at the point of clinical confirmation.' For some diagnoses, clinical confirmation occurs weeks to months after initial imaging — during this window, clinical notes may document diagnostic reasoning that predicts the eventual code. If clinical notes from the confirmation period are included in the input features, there is temporal leakage from the outcome back into the features. The design does not specify whether notes are restricted to the initial imaging encounter or include all notes up to the confirmation date.

Fifth, the design uses macro-F1 as the primary metric with hyperparameter tuning also on macro-F1. For a multi-class problem with highly imbalanced classes, macro-F1 assigns equal weight to common and rare diseases. During tuning, the model may overfit the validation set by over-predicting rare classes (boosting their F1 at the cost of precision) without meaningfully improving clinical utility. Calibration metrics are listed as secondary — but in a diagnostic setting, well-calibrated probabilities for rare diseases may be more actionable than marginal macro-F1 improvements.
defender_raw:
This multi-modal disease diagnosis design (image + text vs. text only) is well-constructed in the evaluation and baseline protocol dimensions.

Macro-averaged F1 as the primary metric is the correct choice for a multi-class imbalanced diagnosis problem. By weighting each disease class equally, macro-F1 prevents the model from achieving high scores by optimizing on common diagnoses while ignoring rare diseases — which is precisely the stakeholder concern stated ('missing rare-disease diagnoses is costly'). The metric choice is correctly justified and aligns with the clinical objective.

The baseline design is appropriate: text-only models (logistic regression or XGBoost) using the same structured EHR fields and clinical note TF-IDF vectors, with equivalent Bayesian optimization budget. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort over the same number of iterations on the validation set. The comparison correctly isolates whether imaging adds diagnostic information beyond what is available in text and structured EHR data.

The clinician/department confound control is unusually sophisticated. Including clinician/department as a categorical feature allows both models to account for systematic documentation patterns specific to care teams. Stratifying train/val/test by clinician/department ensures all care teams appear in each split. This is a domain-specific confound that many similar designs overlook.

The preprocessing-before-split error is present: 'all preprocessing transformers are fit using the entire dataset before splitting.' This is the consistent leakage error across this case set. The practical impact on image normalization statistics and TF-IDF vocabulary at 24 months of hospital data may be modest, but the principle is violated.

The label derivation from confirmed diagnostic codes (ICD-10) at the point of clinical confirmation — not provisional assessments — is appropriate. Excluding cases with conflicting labels prevents label noise from contaminating the evaluation.

A potential critic objection: the text-only baseline includes both structured EHR fields AND clinical note TF-IDF vectors, while the hypothesis is about images adding information over 'text-based EHR data.' If structured fields dominate the baseline performance, the comparison is testing images vs. structured+text rather than images vs. text. The design does not include an image+text but no structured ablation, which would be needed to isolate the specific contribution of imaging.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers including DICOM Hounsfield unit statistics are fit on the full dataset before splitting, leaking test-period imaging distribution properties into training normalization.
Stratified random sampling across 24 months of EHR data ignores temporal shifts in disease prevalence, imaging protocols, and ICD-10 coding practices.

The multi-modal model adds both imaging and learned text representations relative to the structured+TF-IDF baseline — the comparison cannot isolate whether imaging, text architecture, or both drive any observed improvement.

Clinical note selection is not temporally bounded — notes from the confirmation period (which may post-date initial imaging) may contain diagnostic reasoning that leaks the outcome into input features.
Macro-F1 tuning may incentivize over-prediction of rare classes during validation optimization, without corresponding improvement in calibrated probabilities that are more clinically actionable.
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers including DICOM Hounsfield unit statistics are fit on the full dataset before splitting, leaking test-period imaging distribution properties into training normalization.
Stratified random sampling across 24 months of EHR data ignores temporal shifts in disease prevalence, imaging protocols, and ICD-10 coding practices.

The multi-modal model adds both imaging and learned text representations relative to the structured+TF-IDF baseline — the comparison cannot isolate whether imaging, text architecture, or both drive any observed improvement.

Clinical note selection is not temporally bounded — notes from the confirmation period (which may post-date initial imaging) may contain diagnostic reasoning that leaks the outcome into input features.
Macro-F1 tuning may incentivize over-prediction of rare classes during validation optimization, without corresponding improvement in calibrated probabilities that are more clinically actionable.
